text: people are supposed to keep each other safe, keep each other warm. They’re supposed to keep each other. Frankie's quick exit is sign enough he never got the memo. But when Lisa first saw him, abandoned on the shoulder of the highway, he walked into her open arms and rubbed his cheek against her chin in pure gratitude. From the start, he was a smiley gent with a swaggering sort of walk that made his poofy tail swish like a charmed snake. Playtime was always well-received, as was quiet time, for which he’d snuggle up in Lisa’s lap and softly knead her thighs. He ticked all the boxes right away...except for one. In all the weeks she spent with Frankie before putting him up for adoption at the cafe, all the nights he slept in a ball against the small of her back, she didn’t hear one rumble in his throat or huff of vibratory breath during one of his prolonged yawns. She’d known cats that weren't big purrers, but they were usually injured or traumatized in some way, and Frankie didn’t act like he’d experienced either. He was bold. He was sweet. He was very nearly perfect. Lisa didn’t know why it made her so angry. Or so depressed. But she took comfort in knowing she wasn’t alone in it. Frankie’s introductory Instagram post, which featured the large orange feline in a photoshopped top hat and bowtie, brought swarms of people into the cafe, squealing, “Where's the new one?” and “Lemme see that cutie you posted!” and “Please tell me you still have that big ginger boy.” Even with his defect, she thought it would be a matter of days, if not hours, before Frankie met his forever home. When he pranced over to show off the goods, people lost their minds. Even the most tolerant cat would’ve dashed away from a crowd of screaming humans with their wriggling fingers reaching out all at once, but not Frankie. He happily offered his chin and cheeks and fluffy buff tummy, and children begged their parents through torrential tears to let them take the kitty home. So many families wanted to spend time with him that Lisa scheduled a week’s worth of sessions the first day. But within minutes, the excitement every group had at the start of the session vanished. Despite all his other signs of affection, the cat’s refusal to purr upset them, even angered them. Several customers left abruptly, their sandwich half eaten and Frankie still standing with eyes closed and ears flattened in preparation for a head pat that would never come. Lisa still assumed he'd get adopted. How important was purring, anyway, especially when the cat had so much else to offer? He was a handsome creature, fluffy and smiley and cuddly: a real catch to anyone who didn't know better. But everyone knew better, and by the time the Governor forced her to shutter the cafe, Frankie was the only foster left. She tried to make the best of it. She invited him to be one of the venerated house-cats for as long as quarantine lasted. But the relationship couldn’t be one-sided. He had to pull his weight, and that meant loving her the way she needed. Using all manner of toys and catnip, blankets and brushes, she worked tirelessly to earn the cat’s love, like there was a magical combination to unlock his purr and make her mama to the greatest lap cat ever born. But it eventually became clear that Frankie didn’t want Lisa to earn his love. Try as she might, the cat simply wouldn’t purr. Not didn’t , she insisted. Wouldn’t . Spite grew thorns in the otherwise soft bouquet of Lisa’s savior complex, and the rage that followed shredded the pillowy blooms in a way she hadn't felt in years. Frustration, grief, and guilt, sure, but not spite. Frankie's many rejections brought it all back, like reopening old boils that leaked new ichor. The culmination of all her anger and jealousy colonized her once again and soon ran out of places to go. Escaping the confines of her body, it arched behind her like a stranger reading over her shoulder. It told her she wasn’t the problem and she was doing the right thing. How was she supposed to know she was bringing a cursed animal into her home? Squinting at Frankie she whispered. “Is that it? Are you cursed?” The cat stretched out his neck, his nose twitching, and just when she’d closed the distance between them, he released a massive sneeze that sprayed mucus across her hand, shirt, and face. Lisa growled through gritted teeth. The damn cat was cursed, and so was she. Come to think of it, she hadn’t heard anything about Covid-19 until after she rescued Frankie from the highway. Maybe she should’ve left him for the Fates and Ford Broncos to deal with. “This is all your fault,” she hissed. His whiskers splayed like she’d finally won his interest, but he abruptly spun around, giving her a perfect view of his puckered rear end. With an intensity she’d felt only once before, rage knotted up in her blood, causing explosions of pain and desperation that made her scream and stomp and close her hand around a mug boasting “World’s Best Cat Mom,” which she mercilessly hurled at her foster cat’s head. It landed short, the shattered porcelain sending Frankie skittering down the hall, but scaring him wasn’t enough. He needed to feel the terror and confusion she'd felt every day since he cursed the Ninth Life Café. The further she pursued him through the house, the further her sanity unhinged. She threw remotes and pillows and books and coasters, up and down the stairs and all the way to the kitchen where Frankie skittered out the flap on the door. She followed him out, screaming, “Good! Get out of here!” and he stopped, his tail swishing as he turned back to the house. “No
